questionText: Disprove the statement: ``Every integer greater than $1$ can be written as the sum of two prime numbers.''

steps.description: Read the claim as a universal statement.
steps.workingLatex: \forall\ n > 1:\ n = p + q \text{ with } p, q \text{ prime}
steps.explanation: The claim covers *all* integers above $1$ (Goldbach's conjecture is only about even numbers); one integer that is not such a sum disproves it.
We need a single integer that cannot be split into two primes.
An odd sum of two primes needs one even prime, namely $2$, so $n$ would have to equal $2 + (\text{prime})$; if $n-2$ is composite, $n$ fails.

steps.description: Choose an odd number with n − 2 composite.
steps.explanation: Take $n=11$; then $n-2 = 9$, which is composite, so the only chance $2+9$ fails.
We list the ways to write $11$ as an ordered sum and test whether both parts are prime.
In every splitting one part ($9,8,6$ or $4$) is composite, so no pair of primes sums to $11$.

steps.description: Confirm 11 is not such a sum.
steps.explanation: Since every candidate pair fails, $11$ cannot be written as a sum of two primes.

steps.description: Note why odd numbers are the weak point.
steps.explanation: The only way an odd number is a sum of two primes is $2+(\text{prime})$, but $11-2=9$ is composite, sealing the failure.
The integer $11>1$ is not a sum of two primes, so the claim is disproved. (Goldbach's conjecture concerns only *even* integers.)

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Take $n=11$: the splittings $2{+}9,\,3{+}8,\,5{+}6,\,7{+}4$ each contain a composite number, so $11$ is not a sum of two primes. Hence the statement is false. $\blacksquare$
